meta.name: OCI-R Obsessive-Compulsive Inventory - Revised
meta.description: The OCI-R is an 18-item self-report scale measuring OCD symptoms across six dimensions.
meta.author: Foa, Huppert, Leiberg, et al. (2002)
meta.reference: Foa EB, et al. Psychol Assess. 2002;14(4):485-496.
meta.instruction: For each statement, indicate how much it has distressed or bothered you during the past month.

questions.text: I have saved up so many things that they get in the way
I check things more often than necessary
I get upset if objects are not arranged properly
I feel compelled to count while I am doing things
I find it difficult to touch an object when I know it has been touched by strangers
I find it difficult to control my own thoughts
I collect things I do not need
I repeatedly check doors, windows, drawers, etc.
I get upset if others change the way I have arranged things
I feel I have to repeat certain numbers
I sometimes have to wash or clean myself simply because I feel contaminated
I am upset by unpleasant thoughts that come into my mind against my will
I avoid throwing things away because I am afraid I might need them later
I repeatedly check gas and water taps and light switches after turning them off
I need things to be arranged in a particular way
I feel that there are good and bad numbers
I wash my hands more often and longer than necessary
I frequently get nasty thoughts and have difficulty getting rid of them

ranges.label: Non-Clinical Range
ranges.description: Your score is within the non-clinical range. This indicates that your anxiety levels are within the normal range, and your psychological state is relatively calm.
suggestions: Continue maintaining healthy routines
Practice mindfulness
Continue maintaining your healthy lifestyle and coping strategies
Practice daily relaxation activities such as deep breathing or stretching
Keep up a regular exercise routine

ranges.label: Mild OCD Symptoms
ranges.description: Your score suggests mild OCD symptoms. Clinical cutoff is around 21. Mild tension or worry is common in everyday life. Simple self-regulation techniques can often help manage these feelings.
suggestions: Monitor symptoms
Learn about OCD
Practice response prevention
Consider consulting a professional
Learn and practice progressive muscle relaxation

ranges.label: Moderate OCD Symptoms
ranges.description: Moderate OCD symptoms causing distress. At this level, you may find it difficult to relax, experience frequent worry, and notice effects on sleep or concentration.
suggestions: Seek professional assessment
ERP is gold standard treatment
Avoid reassurance-seeking
Consider medication
Consider consulting a counselor or psychiatrist

ranges.label: Severe OCD Symptoms
ranges.description: Severe OCD symptoms impairing daily functioning. Your anxiety level is elevated and may be accompanied by physical discomfort and persistent tension. Speaking with a professional is recommended.
suggestions: Seek specialized treatment
ERP therapy most effective
SSRIs first-line medication
Join support group
OCD is treatable

hoarding.label: Low
hoarding.description: Minimal hoarding. This indicates that your anxiety levels are within the normal range, and your psychological state is relatively calm.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your healthy lifestyle and coping strategies
Practice daily relaxation activities such as deep breathing or stretching
Keep up a regular exercise routine
Cultivate mindful awareness habits
Maintain strong social connections

hoarding.label: Moderate
hoarding.description: Moderate hoarding. Mild tension or worry is common in everyday life. Simple self-regulation techniques can often help manage these feelings.
suggestions: Learn and practice progressive muscle relaxation
Try 5-10 minutes of mindfulness meditation daily
Reduce caffeine and stimulant intake
Record situations that trigger anxiety and try to analyze them rationally
Exercise regularly, at least three times a week

hoarding.label: High
hoarding.description: Significant hoarding. Your anxiety level is elevated and may be accompanied by physical discomfort and persistent tension. Speaking with a professional is recommended.
suggestions: Schedule a professional assessment as soon as possible
A combination of medication and psychotherapy may be beneficial
Learn to recognize and manage panic attacks
Avoid avoidance behavior; gradually face fears under professional guidance
Ensure you have a reliable support system
